body:
Currently the lsp requires an explicit option of `"config"` to be set in the client configuration for a configuration file to apply:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/config.rs#L157-L159

And when there is something impacted in the environment, like when the client configuration or the configuration file iteself changes, then the language server processes an update:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/language_server.rs#L524

If we have automatic behaviour to identify the file when `deno run`, then it only seem logical that the behaviour would be similar by default for the language server, not requiring explicit configuration, which mirrors the command line today, and I would assume that delivery of that functionality would be part of this feature, since a fair amount of people utilise the language server.

Generally this should be fairly straight forward with just a "workspace" and looking in the "workspace root", but when you have "workspace folders" (multi-root workspaces), I suspect it could get a bit convoluted.

Side note, I just realised we are only watching JSON files in the workspace (and not JSONC):

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c487b7ed54a36edcf324005a6ff6e76663544e06/cli/lsp/language_server.rs#L722-L728

And not watching for changes in `.jsonc` files, but if a configuration file is identified, it can be registered for changes, I believe.

Another discussion at one point, which could be delivered later, is that if we automatically detect a `deno.json`/`deno.jsonc` it would imply that the project/workspace was implicitly Deno "enabled" not requiring explicit enablement as it does today.

This still isn't right because I'm using `diagnosticsMessagesToIgnore=1` and the numbers I'm getting are 287ms instead of 200ms. But I think it's getting closer to quick-lint-js's benchmark.

```
bench: 287,436,395 ns/iter (+/- 12,993,664)
```
author: ry

body:
> This still isn't right because I'm using `diagnosticsMessagesToIgnore=1` and the numbers I'm getting are 287ms instead of 200ms. But I think it's getting closer to quick-lint-js's benchmark.
> 
> ```
> bench: 287,436,395 ns/iter (+/- 12,993,664)
> ```

Well, it will be 200ms + tsc diagnostic request time + maybe blocking time if there is some other request in tsc.  The whole purpose of the debounce was to allow other "high priority" tsc requests to get in front of the diagnostic request, but requests are processed in the order they are received:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/eda6e58520276786bd87e411d0284eb56d9686a6/cli/lsp/tsc.rs#L100-L111

Given the test case though I can't specifically think of another request that might be going on, but it could be.

I did check on a reasonably sized project and I am getting:

|measurement|time|
|-|-|
| update_diagnostics_deps | 10ms |
| update_diagnostics_lint | 10ms |
| update_diagnostics_ts | 69ms |

So getting 287ms for a tsc diagnostics publish on a large file is not unreasonable or unexpected.

The first one being < 200ms I believe is actually another side effect all together of a design limitation of the test harness. We tried and tried to find a non-blocking way to "poll" for messages, but haven't, so any unread unsolicited messages will be read and returned, but if you try to poll for a message that isn't there, it will just hang.  So you are likely reading something that was previously sent.

body:
As @andreubotella this PR will start crashing after https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13387 is merged. The proper fix is to store a `v8::Global<v8::Value>` on `MainWorker`, that will point to `globalThis`. This global should be created just after bootstrapping a worker. Then when `load` and `unload` events are dispatched they should call `dispatchEvent` method directly on a `v8::Local<v8::Value>` obtained from the stored global, instead of using `self.js_runtime.execute_script()`.

EDIT: Another solution suggested by Andreu:
```
btw, since fields of the global are accessible at all times from temporary scripts, you could just do dispatchEvent(new Event('load'))
```

Are we really sure we want to do this... All "diagnostic" output of a test goes to stderr, including error messages.  This means that every lsp test will output some 10 lines or so of "setup" information, even if there isn't an error.

> 
> Wouldn't a better solution be to have a flag that goes to the `LSPClient` that enables or disables it?

That's a valid point, I just run the tests locally and indeed there's a lot of output being printed. To be honest it's quite surprising to me, I though that this output would be caught by the test harness and only printed on test failures.
author: bartlomieju

body:
> To be honest it's quite surprising to me, I though that this output would be caught by the test harness and only printed on test failures.

That would make more sense, but personally I don't know how to accomplish that.  The `LSPClient` would hold onto the `stderr` and make it available, but I don't know how to automatically expose it on an assertion failure.  I suspect some sort of "test macro" could work, where if it panics, it dumps the `stderr`.

That being said the "problem" was that there were errors in the logs that were not impacting the outcomes of the tests, which meant in theory the tests weren't actually testing what we thought they were testing.  That wasn't the case in the end, but, the point is valid... always swallowing the `stderr` isn't a good idea, sometimes it should be inspected.

author: benjamingr

body:
@benjamingr
The section 2.8 of DOM spec (ref https://dom.spec.whatwg.org/#observing-event-listeners) says:

> In general, developers do not expect the presence of an event listener to be observable. The impact of an event listener is determined by its callback. That is, a developer adding a no-op event listener would not expect it to have any side effects.

So `addEventListener` shouldn't have any side effects, but we can't implement signal handlers in that way (Listening to signals remove the default behavior of getting that signal). We think implementing signal handler like
```
window.addEventListener("SIGINT", handler);
```
will break/reduce the conformance to the web spec. So we chose this non-standard name

More context: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/11158#issuecomment-871186326

body:
There is a change in behaviour in `contains_specifier()`, specifically in calling `get()` that isn't ever needed by the `op_exists`, which is why I broke it out.  `op_exists` really only looks for local files on disk, mostly probing.  Therefore it abandons the locking of the `file_system_docs` and the calculation of the "freshness" of the document before returning a result.  It simply determines if the file exists on the local file system (or is open in the editor).  "op_exists" and therefore "exists()" are not called by tsc when resolving modules, it is only for probing the filesystem for things like the existance of package.json and other environmental files which might affect its module resolution, which we override anyways.  Very seldom does the file even exist and even more rarely the file is read.

While the other two locations where we use `contains_specifier()`, there is dependence on the "freshness" of the file and likelihood the file will will be otherwise accessed in subsequent calls.

We can make the comments more clear, but I think if we change `contains_specifier()` to the same logic, we will end up with some esoteric edge cases... `exists()` is really only a low level optimisation of `op_exists`, because when opening a file now in 4.5, tsc will issue ~1000 op_exists requests for files that almost never exist in a Deno context and locking the `file_system_docs` just to find out the file doesn't exist is quite unnecessary.
author: kitsonk

@kitsonk I see.

> While the other two locations where we use contains_specifier(), there is dependence on the "freshness" of the file and likelihood the file will will be otherwise accessed in subsequent calls.

This is ok. The `file_system_docs` collection is intended to just help prevent loading the same document multiple times across the `Documents` collection and snapshots. Also, I renamed the method to `exists` as that seems to be how it's used in all cases.

Hey @lucacasonato, thanks for taking the time to explain 🙌 . I'm sorry I've written such a long explanation for the misunderstanding 😅 

With regards to the problem you describe, I strongly agree with your assessment here:

> It would be confusing if async and sync environments behaved differently in this regard to termination.

Considering we'd like the above to be true, I think indeed the only choices would be to (1) either make individual tests the runner's unit of execution or simply (2) not to run the rest of the tests in a file if one of them times out.

IMO (2) could be a bit frustrating as that's different from most runners out there and could make debugging/fixing take longer. On the other hand, what you said about executing the global scope only once per module being less confusing also makes a lot of sense. Personally, I'm slightly more inclined towards (1), but I think your opinion here would be much more valuable as you're more well-informed given you've been much more involved with the project.

What's your preference here?

Do you have any thoughts on what should be Deno's philosophy with regards to testing? I think this might be a case of thinking whether we'd like to give users control and be more hands-off (1) or take control and prevent mistakes ourselves(2).

Please let me know what you think and how you'd like to proceed in terms of creating separate issues, discussing on Discord, or this PR etc...

Thank you!

body:
I wanted to try this branch out on lmdb-js. Everything built fine, but whenever I attempt to load a NAPI binary using `Deno.core.opSync('op_napi_open', 'test.node')` I get `LoadLibraryExW failed` (running on Windows). This is the case even with a .cpp file with bare minimum NAPI_MODULE_INIT function that returns undefined (a napi_value set to undefined). The only thing I could do to get by this error was explicitly define an extern exported `napi_register_module_v1` function, but I couldn't really get it to interact with the exports, and I don't think a NAPI module is supposed to directly use that function. So I guess I have few questions:

- Should this be working Windows yet?
- Should it work to compile my C/C++ code with node-gyp and run on Deno? (or are there ABI incompatibilities?)
- Should I still expect NAPI_MODULE_INIT (or NODE_API_MODULE with node-addon-api) to be the main entry?
- Is there any other debugging that can be done to determine what is wrong with `LoadLibraryExW` failed? I did notice that it takes about one second for it to fail; not sure if that long of time to failure indicates a timeout somewhere (waiting for a callback?)
- Could you point me to any working examples of NAPI module that is working with this branch?

Anyway, thank you for your work on this, looking forward to trying this out.
author: kriszyp

body:
hi @kriszyp 👋  thanks for looking into it

I'm getting similar error on macOS with lmdb 2.3.0-alpha.1. It seems fail to instantly for me with `dyld[9373]: missing symbol called`. This PR does not implement weak references yet so there is a chance I forgot to define a stub symbol.

> The only thing I could do to get by this error was explicitly define an extern exported napi_register_module_v1 function, but I couldn't really get it to interact with the exports, and I don't think a NAPI module is supposed to directly use that function

Oh, IIRC `napi_register_module_v1` is the _new way_ to define an entry point for NAPI modules, it's used in [napi-rs](https://napi.rs/) and [neon](https://neon-bindings.com/). The other way is `napi_module_register` (which I think the `NAPI_MODULE_INIT` macro generates for you in C/C++)

> Should this be working Windows yet?

yup. It should - I haven't tested on Windows since the last rebase :-)

> Should it work to compile my C/C++ code with node-gyp and run on Deno? (or are there ABI incompatibilities?)

everything should work as compiled for Node.

> Should I still expect NAPI_MODULE_INIT (or NODE_API_MODULE with node-addon-api) to be the main entry?

both should work. The current implementation tries to look for `napi_register_module_v1` and falls back to the `NAPI_MODULE_INIT` way.

> Is there any other debugging that can be done to determine what is wrong with LoadLibraryExW failed? I did notice that it takes about one second for it to fail; not sure if that long of time to failure indicates a timeout somewhere (waiting for a callback?)

Hmm, I'm not near a Windows machine atm. (to debug, I just attach `lldb` and let it crash, then move up the frames). Will take a look at the issue.

> Could you point me to any working examples of NAPI module that is working with this branch?

`test_napi/` has unit tests and `test_napi/third_party_tests/test.js` has some real world tests. Run `yarn` in `test_napi/third_party_tests` and  then `cargo test -p test_napi`

body:
One other issue I noticed is how the `process.versions.modules` is reported in Deno's `compat` mode. I recognize that Deno is trying to closely match Node's behavior with the `process.versions` object and the versions reported, which is fine and good, but I don't think it should extend to `process.versions.modules` since this is specifically reporting the ABI version of the NAN/V8 interface, which Deno specifically does _not_ export or support. When I use prebuild/node-gyp-build to load the binary, this looks to determine if a binary compiled for a specific Node ABI can be used, and if `process.versions.modules` is "93" (as reported in Deno), it will load the NAN binary, but this is where we want the loader to be aware that NAN is _not_ available, and to fall back to the NAPI binary (`process.versions.napi` is properly reported as "8", which is a bit of a fib in the main branch, but significantly more truthful in this branch). I'd suggest removing `process.versions.modules` or making it null or empty or something.

BTW, are there built binaries outputted/stored from any of the builds done on this branch  that I can access or should I wait for the experimental release? (I have tried building this branch locally on 2 windows machines, one mac, and one linux and they have all failed to build or run the unit tests. But I can certainly wait for a working build if it is coming).

I thought about it when I paired with @ry and he argued against it.  It does add complexity to it. Is there a compelling case where someone would want to disable it, other than "because they can"? author: kitsonk

body: I could imagine that one has an nginx in front of Deno that is responsible for on the fly encoding. In that case you want Deno to leave the responses as "pure" as possible. I don't think we per-se _need_ to add this for the PR to land, but I think it would be useful to give users an opt-out. The implementation complexity seems rather trivial.
author: lucacasonato

body:
> Also do we need to have special handling for `Range`?

Actually, after doing a bit of research, if a `Content-Range` header is present in the response, we should disable automatic compression, as the user HTTP server has processed a range request, with the range being based off the uncompressed range headers it negotiated.

body:
> > Thank you @bartlomieju, it seems fine now. Is it better to also add test for the described behavior in Windows?
> 
> That would be great! I'll try to get this landed for 1.20

Test for Windows behavior added and CI seem fine. I leave here a quick reference about the tests implemented:

- `netTcpReuseAddr` opens a socket using the default setting for **SO_REUSEADDR** of each OS, then the socket is closed, 
putting it in the **TIME_WAIT** state; a second socket is successfully opened
- `netTcpNoReuseAddr` performs the same steps of the previous test but **SO_REUSEADDR** is set to `false`, in order to test the behavior in OS different from Windows. In this case the test has to fail with an `AddrInUse` error. This test can fail in two point, proving the same property, the first point being the first listener creation (it can fail since sockets from previous tests that occupied the required port are in the **TIME_WAIT** state) while the second point is the creation of the second listener (since the first one is in the **TIME_WAIT** state)
- `netTcpTrueReuseAddrWindows` set **SO_REUSEADDR** to `true` and is run only in Windows. In this case two listeners are bound to the same port, which is usually forbidden for the default setting of **SO_REUSEADDR** equal to `false`

The tests `listenTlsWithReuseAddr`, `listenTlsWithNoReuseAddr` and `listenTlsWithTrueReuseAddrWindows` follow the same 
logic but are for `listenTls`

body:
The changes introduced up to now concern only TCP sockets. With the last two commits I have extended the possibility of using the **SO_REUSEADDR** even for UDP sockets. Since they have a different behavior compared to TCP sockets, I have also updated the docstring of the **reuseAddress** optional argument. This argument now can be specified through `Deno.listenDatagram`. In addition to that, 2 new test have been added: `netUdpReuseAddr` and `netUdpNoReuseAddr`. 

To enable this option, the Socket2 crate has been used, using the same settings of the [std::net](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/6343edfa11c80ffed02de7e059178d4c1637ecdb/library/std/src/sys_common/net.rs) implementation of `UdpSocket`.

Similarly to node ([win udp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/win/udp.c), [unix udp](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/e46c680bf2b211bbd52cf959ca17ee98c7f657f5/deps/uv/src/unix/udp.c)), the options always defaults to `false`, and for both Windows and Linux setting **SO_REUSEADDR** actually uses that option under the hood. For the other *nix systems **SO_REUSEPORT** is actually used.

body:
I have rebased this PR.

I have removed the `reuseAddress` option for TCP sockets. For TCP, this option is unconfigurably `true` on all platforms but Windows. This behaviour matches Node.js and Go. Neither of these systems support disabling `SO_REUSEADDR` on socket listeners.

For UDP, `reuseAddress` is supported. It has the same port stealing behaviour on all systems we support, but to achieve this it uses different syscalls on different platforms. macOS and other BSDs use `SO_REUSEADDR`, while Linux and Windows use `SO_REUSEPORT`. This behaviour matches libuv.

As a follow up PR we can add a `reusePort` option to enable the kernel connection load  balancing behaviour found in Linux, for TCP sockets.

I think this PR is now good to go.

body:
For `Deno.core.refOp` and `unrefOp`, currently if something other than a `PromiseId` is passed, the Rust binding never gets called because that id is not in the promise map. However, in this PR it's directly passed to the binding, which tries to convert it into a `PromiseId`, and throws if it can't.

Combined with changing `PromiseId` to be a type alias of `u32` rather than `i32`, this seems to break the following test: https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/tests/unit/ref_unref_test.ts

Edit: Turns out this is not why the test was breaking. It seems like any numbers passed to `Deno.core.{un,}refOp` –whether they fit in `u32`, `i64` or even if they're infinities or `NaN`– will not throw, but they will get cast in perhaps weird ways. If this is an issue, the arg could be cast into a `v8::Uint32` instead.

body: My original idea was to take the `JoinHandle`s and collect them in a per-isolate vec so they can all be joined after the event loop finishes (since at this point all of these workers will have closed or been terminated, after dropping the `WorkerThread` struct). However, simply ignoring the `JoinHandle`s seems to work just fine. This works even in cases where a panic happens in the worker thread, because the panic handler will kill the process, so there's no need to propagate the panic to the main thread. body:
Fails the following WPT tests:

```
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 100"
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 199"      
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.worker.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 100"
  "/fetch/api/response/response-error.any.worker.html - Throws RangeError when responseInit's status is 199"
```

If we cared for marketing reasons to pass as much WPT tests as possible, then we could just allow status code 101 or 101-198.

I think there's also a better argument to be made for allowing *only* 101:
* status codes 100/102/103 are useless in our current model, because these status codes indicate that the server is going to send *another* response *in addition to* the 1xx response, but in our API there is no support for sending multiple `Response`s for a single `Request`.
* status codes 104-199 don't exist
author: piscisaureus
